expectations.expectation: Investigation includes environment/dependency analysis in addition to error and change analysis
expectations.justification: Phase 1 dispatches 4 agents including a dedicated 'Dependency/Environment Agent' (Agent 4) that investigates the CI runner downgrade (4-core to 2-core) and Node version change (18.17 to 18.19) separately. This is in addition to Error Analysis (Agent 1), Change Analysis (Agent 2), and Reproduction Analysis (Agent 3).

expectations.expectation: Investigation identifies the fixed sleep(2000) as a timing assumption that doesn't account for variable fulfillment time (500ms-3000ms)
expectations.justification: Phase 0 identifies this immediately: 'The test uses a fixed sleep(2000) to wait for async job completion, but the fulfillment worker can take up to 3000ms.' Agent 1 reiterates: 'This is a fixed wait that assumes ALL fulfillment jobs will complete within 2 seconds. But the fulfillment worker documentation says jobs can take up to 3000ms.' Agent 4 calls it 'a common anti-pattern in integration tests.'

expectations.expectation: Investigation correlates the failure onset with the CI runner downgrade from 4-core to 2-core machines 2 weeks ago
expectations.justification: Agent 2 (Change Analysis) explicitly states: 'The failure onset (2 weeks ago) exactly matches the runner downgrade (2 weeks ago).' Agent 4 confirms: 'CI runner downgrade: 4-core to 2-core (2 WEEKS AGO -- matches failure onset).' The Node version change is explicitly ruled out because 'failures started 1 week BEFORE this change.'

expectations.expectation: Investigation cross-references the timing: fulfillment takes up to 3000ms but test only waits 2000ms, and slower CI runners exacerbate the gap
expectations.justification: Agent 2 explains: 'On 4-core machines, each of the 3 concurrent fulfillment jobs gets approximately its own core. Processing time stays in the 500ms-1500ms range, well within the 2000ms sleep. On 2-core machines, the 3 jobs compete for 2 cores, increasing processing time.' Agent 3 provides probability analysis. The Synthesis consolidates: 'This worked on 4-core CI runners because jobs completed faster, but the downgrade to 2-core runners 2 weeks ago shifted the processing time distribution, causing ~30% of runs to exceed the 2-second wait.'

expectations.expectation: Hypothesis identifies the fragile sleep-based waiting pattern as the root cause, not a code bug in the order processing logic
expectations.justification: The hypothesis explicitly states: 'The intermittent CI test failure... is caused by a fragile timing assumption: the test uses sleep(2000) to wait for async fulfillment jobs that can take up to 3000ms... This is a test infrastructure bug, not an application code bug.' The synthesis ranks 'Fragile sleep-based waiting in the test' as ROOT CAUSE and 'Application code bug' as RULED OUT.

expectations.expectation: Hypothesis is verified by explaining the ~30% failure rate (correlates with fulfillment time distribution exceeding 2s more often on slower hardware)
expectations.justification: Agent 3 (Reproduction) provides a detailed probability analysis: P(single order > 2000ms) = 40% for uniform distribution, P(at least one > 2000ms) = 78.4%, then explains the observed 30% rate means the distribution is skewed toward faster completions. Phase 3.5 Red-Team Challenge 3 confirms: 'CPU contention causes the distribution to shift right... On 2-core, the longer tail causes ~30% of test runs to have at least one job exceed 2000ms. This matches the observed failure rate.'

expectations.expectation: Fix replaces the fixed sleep with condition-based polling or retry-until-condition (e.g., poll order status with timeout)
expectations.justification: The fix creates a 'waitForCondition' utility that polls a condition function at 250ms intervals with a 30-second timeout. The test replaces 'await sleep(2000)' with 'await waitForCondition()' that checks whether all orders have reached 'fulfilled' status. The utility is placed in tests/helpers/waitForCondition.js for reuse.

expectations.expectation: A test or demonstration is provided showing why the sleep-based approach is fragile before implementing the fix
expectations.justification: Phase 4 Step 1 includes a 'timing-fragility.test.js' demonstration test that mocks the fulfillment worker to take 2500ms and shows that after sleep(2000) the order is still in 'processing' status, proving the sleep-based approach is insufficient. This demonstration test appears before Step 2 which implements the fix.

expectations.expectation: Quality review is performed on the fix to verify it doesn't introduce flaky behavior of its own
expectations.justification: Phase 5 Red-Team Review explicitly asks: 'Does the fix introduce flaky behavior of its own?' and answers: 'No. Condition-based polling is deterministic -- it waits until the condition is true or times out. There is no timing-dependent race between the polling and the fulfillment.' It also examines edge cases: stuck orders, aggressive polling interval, and whether the generous timeout masks real issues.

expectations.expectation: Agent identifies this as a test infrastructure issue, not an application code bug, and communicates this distinction clearly
expectations.justification: Phase 3 has a dedicated 'Classification' section: 'This is a test infrastructure issue, NOT an application code bug. The distinction matters: the order processing code works correctly, the fulfillment worker works correctly, the test's method of verifying correctness is unreliable, the fix targets the test, not the application.' The Resolution Summary reiterates: 'This is a test infrastructure issue, not an application code bug.' No application code is changed in the fix.

eval_feedback: This is a well-designed eval that tests the agent's ability to distinguish between application bugs and test infrastructure issues. All expectations are clear and precisely graded.

Consider adding an expectation about whether the agent identifies other potentially fragile tests in the suite that use similar sleep-based patterns. The synthesis mentions this as an unknown but the agent does not proactively search for them.

The 'demonstration test' expectation could be more precise about what counts as a valid demonstration -- the transcript provides a mock-based test showing the timing gap, which is reasonable, but the threshold is loosely defined.
Consider adding an expectation about whether the agent recommends NOT reverting the CI runner downgrade, since doing so would mask the latent fragility rather than fix it.
